title: Quantum viscosity mechanism of the dissipative dynamics in the Dicke model expressed via Lindblad equation of motion
authors: M. E. S. Beck, S. S. Seidov, S. I. Muhkin
abstract: Quantum dissipation is studied in the superradiant phase of the Extended Dicke model. It is demonstrated analytically by quantum mechanical derivation of the Lindblad equation for the Dicke model in the superradiant state coupled to Caldeira-Leggett thermal bath, that the effective viscosity appearing in the semiclassical equations of motion of polaritonic condensate survives in the zero temperature limit T -> 0. The nonzero contribution to viscosity contains prefactor nB (ω, T ) + 1 with the Bose-Einstein function nB of harmonic oscillators in the thermal bath, indicating that virtual excitations of harmonic oscillators in the thermal bath coupled to the polaritons of Dicke model give rise to effective viscosity in the T -> 0 limit. Besides, it is demonstrated analytically, that correct expression for Lindbladian in the superradiant phase should be built using condensate-shifted creation and annihilation operators of the photons and pseudospin operators in Holstein-Primakoff representation of the coupled to photons two-level systems in order the system could relax to its minimum energy in T -> 0 limit due to thermal bath provided effective viscosity.
summary: The paper shows that standard Lindblad equations fail to describe relaxation in the Dicke model's superradiant phase because they do not account for the shift in the energy minimum. By deriving a 'dressed' master equation, the authors prove that the system can relax to its ground state and that a quantum viscosity persists even at absolute zero temperature.
main_problem: The failure of standard 'bare' Lindblad master equations to allow the Extended Dicke model to relax to its true ground state in the superradiant phase, and the origin of quantum viscosity in this regime.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a 'dressed' Lindblad equation, using condensate-shifted operators, is required for proper relaxation to the energy minimum, and show that effective quantum viscosity persists even at zero temperature due to virtual excitations of the thermal bath.
method: Quantum mechanical derivation of the Lindblad equation via coupling the system to a Caldeira-Leggett thermal bath, utilizing Holstein-Primakoff and Bogolyubov transformations, and applying a semi-classical approximation.
model_or_system: The Extended Dicke Model (EDM) describing the interaction between an electromagnetic field and an ensemble of N two-level systems, coupled to a Caldeira-Leggett thermal bath of harmonic oscillators.
key_observables: Effective viscosity, energy dynamics, spin coordinates (Sx, Sy, Sz), and phase space coordinates (q, p).
important_parameters: Ultra-strong coupling (USC) regime, superradiant phase, zero-temperature limit (T -> 0), coupling strength g, and Zeeman energy E_Z.
main_assumptions: Large spin limit (S >> 1), proximity to the energy minimum, neglect of tunneling, and the use of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) to neglect rapidly oscillating inter-ladder transitions.
figures_summary: Figure 1b shows the rotation of energy minima and momentum shift; Figure 3a shows semi-classical spin dynamics aligning with the energy minimum; Figure 3b shows energy dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper first identifies the failure of bare dissipators in the superradiant phase, then provides a formal derivation of a dressed Lindblad equation using a thermal bath, analyzes the resulting quantum viscosity mechanism, and concludes with an appendix mapping the transformed operators.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and cavity QED as it addresses a fundamental correction to the master equation formalism in the ultra-strong coupling regime and reveals a temperature-independent mechanism for quantum viscosity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
